DEPARTMENT OF PATHOLOGY AND LABORATORY MEDICINE
ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY — SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

Patient Name: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [REDACTED]
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Sex: Male
Ordering Physician: [REDACTED], MD (Gastroenterology / Oncology)
Clinical Service: Gastroenterology / Medical Oncology
Specimen Received: [DATE]
Report Finalized: [DATE]

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SPECIMEN IDENTIFICATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Specimen A:
  Accession #: SP-[REDACTED]-A
  Specimen Type: Colonoscopic biopsy — sigmoid colon mass
  Clinical Indication: Endoscopically identified, partially obstructing sigmoid colon mass; rule out malignancy. Patient with new onset hematochezia and altered bowel habits. CT abdomen/pelvis (staging) demonstrated 4.5 x 3.8 cm sigmoid mass with pericolic fat stranding and bilobar hepatic lesions.
  Procedure: Colonoscopy with targeted biopsy
  Procedure Date: [DATE]

Specimen B:
  Accession #: SP-[REDACTED]-B
  Specimen Type: CT-guided core needle biopsy — hepatic lesion, segment VI
  Clinical Indication: Evaluation of dominant hepatic lesion (3.8 cm, segment VI) identified on staging CT in the setting of suspected primary colonic malignancy; rule out metastatic disease vs. primary hepatic malignancy.
  Procedure: CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy, 18-gauge, segment VI liver
  Procedure Date: [DATE]

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CLINICAL HISTORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

58-year-old male presenting with hematochezia, change in bowel habits, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss over 3 months. Colonoscopy revealed a partially obstructing, friable, ulcerated sigmoid mass at 35 cm from the anal verge. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast demonstrates a sigmoid colon primary (4.5 x 3.8 cm), multiple pericolonic lymph nodes (up to 1.4 cm), and four bilobar hepatic lesions (dominant lesion 3.8 x 3.1 cm in segment VI; additional lesions in segments II, IV, and VII). No peritoneal implants or extrahepatic nodal disease identified on imaging. CEA: 187.4 ng/mL. CA 19-9: 412 U/mL. Pertinent medical history: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus. Former smoker, 20 pack-years, quit 8 years prior. No prior history of inflammatory bowel disease, polyp syndromes, or colorectal malignancy. No family history of colorectal cancer.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
GROSS DESCRIPTION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Specimen A — Sigmoid Colon Biopsy:
Received in formalin are multiple irregular fragments of pale tan-pink to hemorrhagic soft tissue, in aggregate measuring 1.2 x 0.8 x 0.3 cm. The tissue is soft, somewhat friable. No polypoid or sessile lesion architecture is retained in the biopsy fragments as received. Representative sections entirely submitted in 2 cassettes, labeled A1 and A2.

Specimen B — CT-Guided Liver Core Biopsy:
Received in formalin are three pale tan-white cylindrical core biopsy fragments, each measuring approximately 1.2-1.4 cm in length and 1-2 mm in diameter, in aggregate measuring 1.4 cm in maximum dimension. The cores are firm, somewhat gritty on sectioning. No gross necrosis identified in the cores, though one fragment demonstrates a slightly mottled tan-yellow appearance. Submitted entirely in 1 cassette, labeled B1.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Specimen A — Sigmoid Colon Biopsy:
Histologic sections show fragments of colonic mucosa infiltrated by and largely replaced by a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma (grade 2/3). The neoplastic glands are arranged in irregular, back-to-back tubular and cribriform architecture with loss of normal mucosal laminar organization. Individual glands are lined by columnar to cuboidal cells with enlarged, elongated, hyperchromatic, pseudostratified nuclei. Nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios are markedly elevated. Prominent intraluminal necrotic debris ("dirty necrosis") is present within tumor gland lumina, a morphologic feature highly characteristic of colorectal adenocarcinoma. Mitotic figures are frequent, including atypical forms (estimated 8-10 mitoses per 10 high-power fields). Focal areas of tumor gland budding are noted at the invasive front. No signet ring cell differentiation is identified. No mucinous component is noted (extracellular mucin comprising less than 10% of tumor volume). The surrounding residual colonic mucosa shows moderate chronic active inflammation and focal erosion. No background features of inflammatory bowel disease or dysplastic polyp are identified in the available sample. Lymphovascular invasion cannot be definitively assessed on the limited mucosal biopsy material.

Specimen B — CT-Guided Liver Core Biopsy:
Sections of the liver core biopsy reveal normal hepatic parenchyma partially replaced by nests and glandular clusters of metastatic adenocarcinoma. The metastatic deposits are composed of irregular, infiltrative glandular structures with a morphology closely recapitulating the colonic primary seen in Specimen A — specifically, the same pattern of back-to-back glands with nuclear pseudostratification, luminal dirty necrosis, and frequent mitoses. The tumor infiltrates between preserved hepatocyte plates and is associated with a desmoplastic stromal response. Focal central necrosis within larger metastatic nests is present. No primary hepatocellular carcinoma morphology (trabecular growth pattern, bile production) is identified. No cholangiocarcinomatous differentiation is seen. No lymphovascular invasion is identified within the core sample, though this is limited by tissue volume. The non-neoplastic hepatic parenchyma shows no significant steatosis, fibrosis, or cirrhosis.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ANCILLARY STUDIES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (performed on Specimen A — sigmoid colon biopsy; confirmatory IHC performed on Specimen B — liver core):

  Colorectal lineage markers:
    CDX2 (clone EPR2764Y):        POSITIVE — strong, diffuse nuclear staining in >90% of tumor cells (Specimens A and B)
    CK20 (clone Ks20.8):          POSITIVE — strong cytoplasmic staining, diffuse pattern (Specimens A and B)
    CK7 (clone OV-TL 12/30):      NEGATIVE — absent staining in tumor cells (Specimens A and B)

  Hepatocellular/other primary markers (Specimen B):
    HepPar-1:                     NEGATIVE
    Arginase-1:                   NEGATIVE
    Napsin A:                     NEGATIVE
    TTF-1 (clone 8G7G3/1):        NEGATIVE

  Mismatch Repair (MMR) Proteins (performed on Specimen A — sigmoid colon biopsy):
    MLH1 (clone ES05):            INTACT (retained nuclear expression in tumor cells)
    MSH2 (clone FE11):            INTACT (retained nuclear expression in tumor cells)
    MSH6 (clone EP49):            INTACT (retained nuclear expression in tumor cells)
    PMS2 (clone A16-4):           INTACT (retained nuclear expression in tumor cells)
    Internal positive controls: Adequate (non-neoplastic lymphocytes and stromal cells show appropriate nuclear staining for all four MMR proteins)

  Interpretation of MMR IHC: Mismatch repair-proficient (pMMR). Loss of any MMR protein expression is NOT identified. Results are consistent with proficient mismatch repair (pMMR) / microsatellite stable (MSS) phenotype.

  Ki-67 (MIB-1, clone MIB-1):    Approximately 60-65% nuclear labeling index in tumor cells, consistent with intermediate-to-high proliferative activity.

MOLECULAR STUDIES — PENDING AT TIME OF INITIAL SIGN-OUT:
  RAS/RAF mutational analysis (PCR-based allele-specific genotyping and/or next-generation sequencing panel) submitted from Specimen A (FFPE blocks): PENDING — results to be reported as addendum.
  HER2 FISH (fluorescence in situ hybridization) for gene amplification: PENDING — to be performed if clinically indicated per oncology request.

  NOTE: Comprehensive next-generation sequencing panel (500-gene panel including TMB and MSI-H analysis) requested by Medical Oncology — submitted to reference laboratory. Results to follow as separate addendum report.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS / FINAL DIAGNOSIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SPECIMEN A — SIGMOID COLON, COLONOSCOPIC BIOPSY:
  ADENOCARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (GRADE 2/3)
  - Glandular architecture with prominent intraluminal dirty necrosis, characteristic of colorectal primary
  - No signet ring cell features; no mucinous differentiation (< 10% extracellular mucin)
  - Immunophenotype consistent with colorectal primary: CDX2+, CK20+, CK7-
  - Mismatch repair protein expression intact (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+)
    Interpretation: PROFICIENT MISMATCH REPAIR (pMMR)
  - RAS/RAF mutational analysis: PENDING (addendum to follow)

SPECIMEN B — LIVER, SEGMENT VI, CT-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY:
  METASTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA, MORPHOLOGICALLY CONSISTENT WITH COLORECTAL PRIMARY
  - Identical glandular morphology to Specimen A (sigmoid colon primary)
  - Intraluminal dirty necrosis; desmoplastic stromal response in liver parenchyma
  - Immunophenotype: CDX2+, CK20+, CK7-, HepPar-1-, Arginase-1-, Napsin A-, TTF-1-
  - Primary hepatocellular carcinoma excluded; primary lung adenocarcinoma excluded
  - Findings are CONSISTENT WITH METASTATIC COLORECTAL ADENOCARCINOMA

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PATHOLOGIST COMMENTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The combined histologic and immunohistochemical findings from Specimens A and B establish a diagnosis of moderately differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous hepatic metastasis. The morphology is classical for colorectal adenocarcinoma, including back-to-back glands, nuclear pseudostratification, frequent mitoses, and intraluminal dirty necrosis. The CDX2-positive, CK20-positive, CK7-negative immunophenotype strongly supports a lower gastrointestinal tract primary and effectively excludes hepatocellular carcinoma, primary lung adenocarcinoma, pancreatobiliary adenocarcinoma (typically CK7+/CK20-/CDX2-), and upper GI adenocarcinoma.

Retained expression of all four mismatch repair proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) by immunohistochemistry indicates a proficient MMR (pMMR) phenotype. This result predicts a microsatellite stable (MSS) tumor, which is associated with a lower likelihood of response to immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy and confirms the tumor is not Lynch syndrome-associated by protein expression criteria. However, formal germline testing should be considered in appropriate clinical contexts.

KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF mutational analysis results are pending and will be issued as an addendum. These results are critical for guiding anti-EGFR therapy eligibility (cetuximab, panitumumab). Comprehensive NGS panel results will also be reported separately by the reference laboratory.

This case was reviewed by two staff pathologists.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ADDENDUM (Issued [DATE]):
  RAS/RAF Mutational Analysis (pyrosequencing / allele-specific PCR, Specimen A FFPE):
    KRAS Exon 2 (codons 12/13):   MUTATION DETECTED — p.G12D (c.35G>A)
    KRAS Exon 3 (codon 61):       Wild-type
    KRAS Exon 4 (codons 117/146): Wild-type
    NRAS Exon 2 (codons 12/13):   Wild-type
    NRAS Exon 3 (codon 61):       Wild-type
    NRAS Exon 4 (codons 117/146): Wild-type
    BRAF Exon 15 (codon 600):     Wild-type (V600E not detected)

  Summary: KRAS p.G12D mutation identified. NRAS wild-type. BRAF V600E wild-type. RAS/RAF mutation status precludes use of anti-EGFR antibody therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab). Results correlated with comprehensive NGS panel.

  HER2 FISH (Specimen A): Copy number 2.1 (ratio 1.05). HER2 NOT AMPLIFIED by FISH criteria.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
